name: Celtic
political_explanation: Celtic's Green Brigade has a well-documented explicit socialist and Irish-republican political identity, including displays of communist iconography and organised Palestinian-solidarity actions that have specifically drawn UEFA sanctions, meaning, like Hellas Verona's Brigate Gialloblu, this finding rests partly on official disciplinary action from football authorities, not just journalistic description. Green Brigade remains Celtic's principal and currently active ultras-style group, prominent and highly visible within Celtic's broader fanbase. This is among the clearest, most extensively documented Left-wing/Hardcore entries in the entire dataset, combining explicit stated ideology, distinctive iconography, concrete political action, and repeated institutional sanctions into a single, well-corroborated political identity.

name: Bayern Munich
political_explanation: Bayern's Schickeria Munchen is widely documented for sustained, explicit anti-fascist activism, most notably a long-running memorial campaign for Kurt Landauer, the club's Jewish pre-war president who was persecuted by the Nazis. This is one of the more institutionally significant entries in the dataset: the Landauer campaign has drawn public acknowledgment from the club itself, not just from the ultras group, giving it an unusually strong evidentiary basis beyond typical ultras-culture reporting. Schickeria Munchen remains Bayern's principal politically active ultras group. The Hardcore coding reflects this sustained, specific, multi-decade campaign rather than a general 'anti-fascist' characterisation of the kind found in some Moderate-tier entries elsewhere in this dataset.

name: Eintracht Frankfurt
political_explanation: Eintracht Frankfurt's Ultras Frankfurt '97 is widely cited as one of German football's most explicitly anti-racist, anti-fascist ultras groups, running consistent, ongoing anti-discrimination campaigns rather than holding a general reputation alone. This sustained, campaign-based activism, comparable in kind to Bayern's Schickeria Munchen, supports the Hardcore coding. Ultras Frankfurt '97 remains Eintracht Frankfurt's principal organised ultras group. This entry is part of a broader, recognisable pattern in this dataset of explicitly and actively anti-fascist German ultras scenes, also including St. Pauli, Union Berlin, Bayern Munich, and Dortmund, suggesting organised anti-fascist ultras politics is a genuinely widespread, not isolated, current within German football specifically.

name: Borussia Dortmund
political_explanation: Dortmund's current, dominant ultras identity, led by The Unity, has a publicly documented anti-fascist, anti-racist platform, and has actively confronted far-right elements within Dortmund's own broader fanbase, which is stronger evidence than groups that are merely anti-fascist in stated identity without documented confrontational action. This is why the club is coded Left-wing/Hardcore overall. Separately, a historic 1980s hooligan firm, Borussenfront, was well-documented as far-right, with a former leading figure later identified at the 2014 HoGeSa demonstration — but this is explicitly historic and distinct from Dortmund's current ultras scene, not a live, competing political current today, which is why it doesn't change the club's overall current categorization.

name: Union Berlin
political_explanation: Union Berlin's Wuhlesyndikat maintains the club's consistent anti-fascist stance, rooted specifically in East Berlin's working-class and Cold War-era dissident history — Union Berlin's fanbase has a well-documented reputation, including in mainstream German journalism beyond ultras-specific sourcing, for having maintained a distinct, quietly oppositional civic identity under the GDR that carried into the club's post-reunification political character. This deeper historical grounding, beyond typical ultras-culture reporting, supports the Hardcore coding. Wuhlesyndikat remains Union Berlin's principal organised ultras group. This is one of several entries in this dataset, alongside Dresden, Magdeburg, and Cottbus, where former East German origins are directly relevant to a club's documented political character, though here pointing in the opposite political direction.

name: Werder Bremen
political_explanation: Werder Bremen presents a genuinely mixed picture that shouldn't be flattened into one label. The club's main current ultras groups, Infamous Youth and Sherwood Youth, are described in German fan-culture sourcing as left-leaning, including a documented friendship with St. Pauli's explicitly far-left ultras scene — this is the more prominent, central fan-culture identity, hence the overall Left-wing/Moderate categorization. However, a separately-named hooligan crew, Endstufe-Crew Bremen, was specifically identified by antifascist monitoring among 'Nazihooligans' at the October 2014 HoGeSa far-right demonstration in Cologne. Both can and do coexist at the same club — a left-leaning ultras scene and a documented far-right hooligan crew are not mutually exclusive, and this entry reflects that real duality rather than picking one and ignoring the other.

name: Borussia Mönchengladbach
political_explanation: Gladbach's ultras scene, including Sottocultura Ultras Gladbach, is described in German fan-culture discussion sources as generally right-leaning. This is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore specifically because the sourcing found was informal (fan-forum discussion) rather than journalistic or institutional, unlike most other Right-wing entries in this dataset, which rest on news reporting, government statements, or dedicated archive sites. This lower-confidence sourcing is an honest limitation worth flagging directly rather than glossing over: the underlying claim may well be accurate, but it hasn't been corroborated by the stronger source types used elsewhere in this project, which is why the scope here is Moderate rather than Hardcore despite a directionally clear claim.

name: 1. FC Köln
political_explanation: Cologne's fan culture, including the Wilde Horde ultras group, is consistently described as carrying an explicit anti-fascist, tolerant identity, broadly consistent with Cologne's progressive civic reputation within Germany. This is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore because the sourcing found leans more toward general cultural characterisation of the city's fan scene than the kind of specific, sustained organisational activism documented for Hardcore-tier German entries like Bayern's Schickeria Munchen or Dortmund's The Unity. Wilde Horde remains 1. FC Koln's principal named ultras group. The underlying lean is genuinely left, just resting on somewhat softer evidence than the Hardcore-tier German entries.

name: Schalke 04
political_explanation: Schalke's Ultras Gelsenkirchen and Marler Jungs are both specifically documented, via a dated 2015 fan-culture archive entry, as having jointly participated in a street blockade that prevented a neo-Nazi rally, organised by the far-right party 'Die Rechte,' from reaching Gelsenkirchen — a claim corroborated even by 'Die Rechte' itself, which complained about the blockade in its own commentary. This specific, dated, cross-confirmed anti-fascist action is stronger evidence than a vaguer 'aligned with a right-leaning club' claim this project initially found and later discarded in favour of this direct finding. Both groups remain part of Schalke's organised hardcore support, and the coding here is Moderate given the single, if well-documented, incident this rests on.

St. Pauli is the explicit reference point several other entries in this dataset cite when describing their own left-leaning friendships or comparisons (Werder Bremen's ultras groups, for instance, are noted as maintaining a friendship specifically with St. Pauli). Ultra Sankt Pauli remains St. Pauli's principal ultras group, and this entry carries the highest possible confidence of any Left-wing/Hardcore case in this dataset.

name: Paris Saint-Germain
political_explanation: PSG's ultras history is genuinely split: historically divided between the far-right-associated Boulogne end and the left-wing, multicultural Auteuil end through the 2000s, before the club dissolved all its ultras groups in 2010 following violence. The current, unified Collectif Ultras Paris, formed in 2016, has organised concrete left-coded solidarity actions, including an aid collection for the 2020 Beirut port explosion, which is real, specific evidence for the current group rather than an inference from the historic, now-superseded Boulogne/Auteuil split. This is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore given the group's relatively recent formation and the absence of a sustained multi-year campaign comparable to Hardcore-tier entries.

name: RC Lens
political_explanation: RC Lens's Red Tigers Lens 1994 are explicitly documented as anti-racist, tied to Lens's historic coal-mining-region working-class identity in northern France's former industrial heartland. As with Saint-Etienne's Green Angels, this is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore because it rests on a specific but single dedicated source rather than the sustained, multi-source pattern found in Hardcore-tier entries. Red Tigers Lens 1994 remains Lens's principal organised ultras group. This entry, together with Green Angels at Saint-Etienne, forms part of a recognisable pattern in this dataset of left-leaning ultras identity specifically tied to French mining-region working-class heritage, distinct from the more explicitly stated far-left politics found in Spain's Rayo Vallecano or Cadiz.

name: Olympique Lyonnais
political_explanation: Lyon's Bad Gones present an important nuance worth stating plainly: a dedicated ultras-archive source explicitly declines to characterise the group's ideology, stating there is 'no single, stable self-declared ideology that sums up its whole history,' a narrower claim about the group's own formal self-definition specifically. Independent journalism is nonetheless extensive and consistent across more than a decade: Spanish outlets have described them as 'known throughout Europe for its far-right ideology,' with reported nationalist/ultra-right characterisation, police treatment as far-right for security purposes, and coverage of Lyon as a far-right hotspot tied to the group. Weighed against one archive site's hedge, this substantial independent press record supports keeping the Hardcore coding, flagged rather than presented as fully settled.

name: RC Strasbourg
political_explanation: The French Interior Ministry itself, in an interministerial circular targeting several ultras groups for potential dissolution, explicitly accused Strasbourg's Offenders group of being 'a neo-Nazi cell disguised as a supporters' identity.' This is a direct accusation from a national government body, among the single strongest categories of evidence in this entire dataset, stronger than journalistic reporting, informal forum sourcing, or even most institutional disciplinary actions, since it represents an official state determination specifically about this group's political character. Offenders is a second, separately-documented group at Strasbourg alongside the club's other listed ultras group, Ultra Boys 90, which carries no comparable documented political finding of its own.

name: Toulouse FC
political_explanation: Toulouse's main ultras group, Indians Tolosa, is itself documented as officially apolitical, displaying genuine anti-racist tifos and strictly excluding French flags, a symbol carrying far-right connotations in French ultras culture. However, the same independent source documents that this apoliticism has enabled tolerance of a separate, named far-right faction, Camside Tolosa, operating within the group's virage, which has hosted visiting neo-Nazi hooligans. This is coded as a Faction-level right-wing finding rather than 'no known position' or a whole-group label: there is real, specific, documented far-right presence, but it is bounded to a distinct named faction rather than reflecting Indians Tolosa's own stated and demonstrated anti-racist identity.

political_explanation: Red Star's supporter group, Red Star Fans, is explicitly documented by French Wikipedia as anti-fascist, in solidarity, and left-anchored ('ancree a gauche'), consistent with the club's own historic identity as a symbol of Paris's 'banlieue rouge' ('red suburb') political tradition, meaning the club's own institutional history, not just its current ultras group, carries this left political identity, similar to how Athletic Bilbao's cantera policy reinforces its ultras group's Basque-nationalist politics. This dual reinforcement supports the Hardcore coding. Red Star Fans remains Red Star's principal organised support group, notably having faced government dissolution threats in 2025 alongside several other French ultras groups researched for this project.

This is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore because, while the anti-fascist characterisation is explicit and direct, the sourcing is a single dedicated piece rather than the kind of sustained, multi-source, institutionally-acknowledged pattern found in the Hardcore-tier entries. Green Angels remains one of Saint-Etienne's two named ultras groups, alongside Magic Fans, not separately categorized here due to insufficient specific documentation of its own political character.

name: Inter Milan
political_explanation: Reporting has linked Inter's historic hardcore ultras faction, Boys San, to far-right political figures and, in some cases, neo-fascist-linked hooligan networks. This is coded as a Faction-level finding because it describes a specific named sub-group within Inter's broader Curva Nord Milano ultras umbrella, which itself carries no comparable documented political stance as a whole — Inter's general fanbase is, if anything, sometimes characterised as more conservative-bourgeois in a general demographic sense than politically far-right in an organised, ultras-specific way. Boys San remains historically significant to Inter's ultras scene, and this entry illustrates the same faction-versus-whole-group distinction applied consistently across similar cases in this dataset.

name: Napoli
political_explanation: Napoli's organised support, including named groups Ultras Napoli 1972 and Fedayn EAM 1979, carries a documented anti-establishment, anti-northern-racism political dimension specifically tied to Southern Italian identity politics — Napoli's ultras scene has organised against discriminatory chants historically directed at Neapolitans by fans of northern Italian clubs, giving this a specific, identifiable political content beyond a generic 'left-wing' label. This is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore because the underlying activism, while real and specific, is more identity-and-anti-discrimination-focused than an explicit stated ideological programme of the kind found in Hardcore-tier entries like Fiorentina's Collettivo Autonomo Viola. Both named groups remain part of Napoli's broader organised ultras scene.

name: AS Roma
political_explanation: Roma's historic ultras group Fedayn was founded in 1972 by Roberto Rulli with explicitly documented left-wing and anarchist tendencies, drawn specifically from Rome's working-class Quadraro and Cinecitta districts, and was, from its founding, the deliberate ideological opposite number to Roma's other historic group, the bourgeois-right 'Boys.' This explicit, founding-era ideological self-positioning, rather than a later or inferred characterisation, is unusually clear evidence, which supports the Hardcore coding. Fedayn remains part of Roma's broader Curva Sud ultras umbrella. This entry illustrates a recurring pattern in Italian ultras history in this dataset, where a single curva can historically contain openly rival left and right factions.

name: Juventus
political_explanation: Juventus's Drughi have been linked to repeated, documented racist incidents, specifically monkey-chanting episodes directed at opposing Black players, occurring across multiple seasons rather than as an isolated event, which is why this is coded Hardcore rather than Moderate despite the absence of an explicit political self-declaration from the group itself. Drughi remain one of Juventus's principal named ultras groups, historically the club's largest. This entry illustrates a distinction maintained throughout this dataset: a documented pattern of racist conduct is treated as sufficient for a Hardcore right-wing coding on its own terms, independent of whether the group has issued any formal ideological statement about itself.

name: Atalanta
political_explanation: Atalanta's 1980s-founded 'Wild Kaos' current, closely matching the club's listed Wild Boys group, is documented as right-leaning, having evolved into association with the regionalist-right Lega Lombarda party, explicitly distinct from an older, separate current at the same club, the far-left Brigate Nerazzurre, which formed in 1976 and displayed Che Guevara imagery. This is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore because the party-association evidence, while specific, describes an evolution over time rather than a fixed, extreme, or violence-linked identity. This entry is another example in the dataset of a single club's ultras history containing genuinely opposed political currents at different points in time, similar to Torino and Real Valladolid.

name: Bologna
political_explanation: Bologna's Curva Andrea Costa carries a documented left-wing, anti-fascist reputation, consistent with the club's location in Emilia-Romagna, one of Italy's most historically left-wing regions. This is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore because the documentation found describes a general reputation rather than a specific campaign, incident, or explicit political self-naming of the kind found in Hardcore-tier Italian entries like Collettivo Autonomo Viola or Fedayn. Curva Andrea Costa remains Bologna's principal organised ultras presence. The regional context is genuinely relevant background here, unlike some other entries in this dataset where regional inference alone was explicitly rejected as insufficient evidence for a specific group.

name: Lazio
political_explanation: Lazio's Irriducibili are among the most notorious far-right ultras groups in European football, with extensively documented fascist and neo-Nazi symbolism, including pro-Mussolini banners and antisemitic taunts directed at opposing fans and players over many years. This is one of the most independently corroborated entries in the entire dataset — Lazio's ultras scene is probably the single most internationally reported case of explicit far-right ultras identity in European football, cited routinely across journalism, academic writing on football and politics, and general football culture writing well beyond the sources specifically consulted for this project. Irriducibili remain Lazio's principal and currently active organised ultras group.

name: Udinese
political_explanation: Udinese's main historic ultras group, Hooligans Teddy Boys (HTB), has a documented history of right-wing and racist actions, including a widely reported 1989 antisemitic incident directed at a new signing, a specific, named, dated incident rather than a general reputation, which supports the Hardcore coding. HTB remains one of Udinese's most historically significant ultras groups. This is among the older documented incidents in this dataset, predating most other specific-incident entries by two to three decades, showing that Udinese's documented far-right ultras history is a long-standing pattern rather than a recent development, consistent with how Italian sources describe the club's broader tifoseria history.

name: Torino
political_explanation: Italian political-mapping journalism specifically describes Ultras Torino as the curva's 'moderate,' more left-oriented FACTION, explicitly contrasted, within the very same source and the very same curva, with a separate far-right splinter group, Torino Hooligans. This is coded as a Faction-level finding rather than a whole-curva label because the source's own framework is explicitly about internal division within Torino's support, not a single unified political character for the club's ultras scene as a whole. Ultras Torino remains one of two named, politically opposed currents documented within the same broader fan base, a useful illustration of why this dataset distinguishes whole-group findings from faction-level ones.

This is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore because the documentation of specific violent or hate-speech incidents tied to Gradinata Nord is thinner than for the Hardcore-tier Italian entries such as Irriducibili, Brigate Gialloblu, and Hooligans Teddy Boys, resting more on a general right-leaning/nationalist characterisation. The direct political self-naming here — 'collettivo autonomo' isn't ultras jargon, it's specifically far-left Italian political terminology — is stronger and more explicit evidence than most other Leftist entries in this dataset, supporting the Hardcore coding. Collettivo Autonomo Viola remains Fiorentina's principal politically identified ultras group. Florence and Tuscany's broader historical association with Italian left politics provides useful context but isn't the basis for this specific group-level finding.

Curva Sud Venezia remains Venezia's principal organised ultras group. This is a genuine, if more modestly evidenced, addition to the broader pattern of left-documented ultras scenes across several Italian clubs in this dataset.

political_explanation: Ascoli's Settembre Bianconero is grouped among the right-leaning groups in a widely circulated Italian newspaper political mapping of Italian ultras scenes, a single, if reputable and widely-referenced, source. This is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore because it rests on inclusion in a general political mapping rather than a specific documented incident, campaign, or explicit self-declaration, unlike the Hardcore-tier Italian entries. Settembre Bianconero remains one of Ascoli's principal historic ultras groups, alongside a more recent splinter group, Ascoli Piceno Ultras, formed in 2005, which isn't separately categorized here due to insufficient specific documentation of its own political character.

name: Barcelona
political_explanation: Barcelona's ultras history centres on Boixos Nois, a hardcore group whose far-right, Catalan-separatist identity was extensively documented before the club formally banned it from the Camp Nou in 2003 over repeated violent and racist incidents. This is a historic, banned faction rather than the club's current, much broader and more centrist-Catalanist fanbase — an important distinction, since the general Barca support base bears no resemblance to Boixos Nois politically. The categorization here reflects the ultras group specifically, which is the only segment of Barcelona's support with a clearly documented political character. Current matchday animation comes from officially sanctioned penyes with no comparable political identity, which is why this describes organised-ultras history, not the club's present-day fanbase.

name: Real Madrid
political_explanation: Real Madrid's historic ultras group, Ultra Sur, carried a documented far-right identity with associations to Francoism, and was formally banned from the Bernabeu by the club in 2013-2014 after years of violent and racist incidents. Independent Spanish sourcing consistently describes this as neofascist in character. As with Barcelona, this categorization applies to the specific, now-banned ultras group rather than Real Madrid's current, officially sanctioned Grada Fans RMCF, composed of club-accredited penyas with no comparable documented ideology. Ultra Sur remains symbolically significant, maintaining an ongoing public conflict with club president Florentino Perez, but functionally no longer occupies the Bernabeu, which is why the club's current matchday support isn't itself categorized as far-right.

name: Atletico Madrid
political_explanation: Frente Atletico is one of the most extensively documented far-right ultras groups in European football, with roughly 2,500 members historically and a decades-long reputation as an explicitly nationalist organisation. Its severity is underscored by a specific, well-documented violent episode: in 1998, a sub-section of the group ('Bastion') murdered a rival Real Sociedad fan, Aitor Zabaleta, near the old Vicente Calderon — one of the clearest cases in European ultras history where a stated far-right ideology and lethal violence are directly linked to a named group. Frente Atletico remains a central, unavoidable part of Atletico's hardcore support today, not merely a historic footnote, which is why this is coded current and Hardcore rather than historic.

name: Real Betis
political_explanation: Betis's main ultras group, Supporters Gol Sur, was explicitly named among Spain's far-right-linked ultras groups by a dedicated investigative piece into the football-ultras roots of Spanish far-right street movements, and a second, independent dedicated ultras-archive source corroborates a far-right association for the group's environment across recent decades. This double sourcing — one investigative journalism piece specifically about far-right movements, one dedicated ultras-archive fact-sheet — is stronger than a single-source claim, which is why this is coded Hardcore. Supporters Gol Sur remains Betis's central, currently active organised ultras group, prominent in the Villamarin's Gol Sur end, not a historic or diminished presence.

name: Celta Vigo
political_explanation: Celta Vigo's main ultras group, Celtarras, carries a documented Galician-regionalist, left-leaning identity, consistent with a broader pattern among Galician and northern Spanish regionalist ultras scenes (also seen at Deportivo). This is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore because the documentation centres on a regional-identity-linked left lean rather than an explicit, stated ideological programme comparable to the Basque abertzale groups or Rayo's self-branded working-class politics. Celtarras remains Celta's principal organised support group. The regionalist framing here reflects genuine, if less intensely stated, left-leaning political character rather than the absence of one — it's a real but softer lean than the Hardcore-tier entries.

name: Getafe

name: Rayo Vallecano
political_explanation: Rayo Vallecano's Bukaneros are among the clearest self-identified far-left ultras groups in Europe. The group explicitly brands itself the 'symbol of the working class,' ties its identity directly to Vallecas's historically left-wing, working-class neighbourhood, and has taken concrete political action consistent with that stated identity, including campaigning to block a player signing over his reported far-right sympathies. This combination of explicit self-branding, neighbourhood-rooted political identity, and demonstrated willingness to act on it makes Bukaneros one of the strongest, least ambiguous Hardcore entries in this entire dataset. Bukaneros remains Rayo's central, currently active ultras group, continuously since its 1992 founding.

name: Valencia
political_explanation: Valencia's main ultras group, Ultras Yomos, has a long-documented right-wing/nationalist reputation within Spain's ultras scene. This is reinforced by direct, personal evidence: a named former leader of the group has publicly self-identified as far-right, including reported use of Falangist salutes and slogans in public settings. This combination — an ultras group with a consistent institutional reputation plus an on-record leadership figure openly embracing far-right symbolism — places Ultras Yomos among the more clearly evidenced Hardcore entries in this dataset, distinct from cases resting only on incident reports or informal forum characterisation. The group remains Valencia's principal organised ultras presence today, not a historic or defunct one.

name: Real Sociedad
political_explanation: Real Sociedad's Herri Norte Taldea carries a left-Basque nationalist identity tied to the broader Basque independence movement, historically even more overtly linked to Basque separatist politics than city rival Athletic Bilbao's equivalent group. This is a core, decades-old identity rather than a recent or contested characterisation — Basque left-nationalist (abertzale) ultras culture is one of the most consistently and multiply documented political currents in Spanish football, also seen at Athletic Bilbao, Osasuna, and Alaves. Herri Norte Taldea remains Real Sociedad's principal ultras group today. The Hardcore coding reflects both the consistency of the sourcing and the explicit, long-standing nature of the group's political identity, not a single incident or recent shift.

name: Espanyol
political_explanation: Espanyol's historic ultras group, Brigadas Blanquiazules, carried a far-right identity documented across multiple Spanish sources, including explicit inclusion in an investigative mapping of far-right-linked ultras groups. The group was officially dissolved in 2010, and matchday animation at the RCDE Stadium is now led by two newer collectives, La Curva and Juvenil 1991, which announced a unified project in 2025 and do not carry the same documented political character. As with Barcelona and Real Madrid, this categorization describes the historic, no-longer-active ultras group rather than the club's present-day organised support, which is a meaningful distinction worth keeping in mind when reading this entry.

name: Athletic Bilbao
political_explanation: Athletic Bilbao's Herri Norte carries the same left-Basque nationalist identity as Real Sociedad's equivalent group, tied to the broader Basque independence movement and reinforced by the club's own cantera (Basque-only recruitment) policy, which embeds Basque identity politics into the institution itself, not just its ultras. This dual reinforcement — an explicitly political ultras group plus a club-level policy rooted in the same regional-nationalist identity — is unusual in this dataset and supports the Hardcore coding. Herri Norte remains Athletic's principal ultras presence, continuously active since 1982. This is one of the more institutionally embedded political identities in the dataset, extending beyond the ultras group alone into the club's own defining policy.

name: Sevilla
political_explanation: Sevilla's Biris Norte has a long-documented left-wing identity within Spanish ultras culture, historically regarded as one of the first major organised ultras groups in Spanish football (founded 1975) and consistently characterised as left-leaning across independent sourcing, in explicit contrast to cross-city rival Real Betis's far-right-documented Supporters Gol Sur. This decades-long consistency, plus the group's foundational status in Spanish ultras history, supports the Hardcore coding. Biris Norte remains Sevilla's principal organised ultras group in the Sanchez-Pizjuan's Gol Norte end. The Sevilla-Betis rivalry is unusually notable in Spanish football specifically because it pairs a left-documented group against a right-documented one across the same city derby.

name: Alaves
political_explanation: Alaves's Indar Gorri, whose name translates from Basque as 'Red Force,' carries the same left-Basque nationalist ('abertzale') identity documented at Osasuna, Athletic Bilbao, and Real Sociedad, extending the same well-established political current to Vitoria-Gasteiz, the Basque Country's capital. The name itself, shared with Osasuna's group, signals the shared abertzale political tradition across these clubs' ultras scenes even though the clubs themselves are unrelated. This is coded Hardcore for the same reason as the other Basque-nationalist entries: the 'abertzale' characterisation is specific, repeated, and consistent with the broader documented pattern, not an isolated or ambiguous claim. Indar Gorri remains Alaves's main organised ultras presence.

name: Levante
political_explanation: Levante's Frente Bukanero carries a documented left-wing, anti-fascist identity, explicitly positioned in contrast to cross-city rival Valencia's right-leaning, far-right-documented Ultras Yomos — another case, like Sevilla-Betis, where a local derby pairs an explicitly left-documented ultras group against an explicitly right-documented one. This direct, named contrast across a single city's two ultras scenes is itself a form of corroboration, since both groups' reputations are documented relative to each other independently. Frente Bukanero remains Levante's main organised ultras group. The Hardcore coding reflects the explicit anti-fascist self-identification found in sourcing, rather than an inferred regional or working-class lean of the kind seen in some Moderate-tier entries elsewhere in this dataset.

name: Osasuna
political_explanation: Osasuna's Indar Gorri is explicitly documented as 'abertzale' (Basque/Navarrese left-nationalist) in Spanish ultras-culture sourcing, placing it within the same well-documented left-Basque nationalist current as Athletic Bilbao, Real Sociedad, and Alaves. The 'abertzale' label specifically ties the group to the far-left strand of Basque/Navarrese nationalism rather than mainstream Spanish left politics, distinguishing it from more generically left-leaning regionalist groups elsewhere in this dataset (e.g. Celta, Deportivo). Indar Gorri remains Osasuna's main organised ultras group in El Sadar's animation end, continuously since 1987. This is coded Hardcore on the strength of the specific, repeated 'abertzale' characterisation across sourcing, not a single ambiguous reference.

name: Racing Santander
political_explanation: Racing Santander's Juventudes Verdiblancas, a dedicated Spanish ultras-archive club guide's central, ongoing group active since the 1980s and continuously significant through the club's institutional crisis in the 2010s, has had its public image 'associated in different periods with the far right,' per that same dedicated source. The hedged, period-specific phrasing, rather than a flat, unqualified 'far-right' label used for other Hardcore entries, is why this is coded Moderate rather than Hardcore: the evidence supports a real right-leaning association without the same degree of consistency or specificity found in, say, Real Madrid's or Atletico's entries. Juventudes Verdiblancas remains Racing's principal, currently active ultras group.

name: Malaga

Frente Bokeron remains Malaga's main and currently active ultras group. The Hardcore coding reflects a specific, direct 'extreme-right' characterisation in dedicated ultras-culture sourcing, rather than an inferred lean — treated with the same weight as the other explicitly-labelled Hardcore entries rather than a softer, incident-based Moderate coding.

name: Cordoba
political_explanation: Cordoba's Brigadas Blanquiverdes sits at a genuine contested point between two sources: one dedicated Spanish ultras-culture site labels the group apolitical, while a separate, more detailed forum source — independently verified elsewhere in this project's research as reliably accurate on dozens of other groups' details — explicitly states 'politically they are nationalist,' using 'nationalist' consistently to mean Spanish-nationalist/right-leaning throughout that source. Given the second source's demonstrated reliability, this is weighted toward Right-wing, but coded Moderate rather than Hardcore because there is no documented pattern of violence or hate speech specifically tied to the group, unlike several other Right-wing Hardcore entries in this dataset. Brigadas Blanquiverdes remains Cordoba's main organised ultras group.

name: Sporting Gijon
political_explanation: Sporting Gijon's Ultra Boys are explicitly documented as extreme-right in dedicated Spanish ultras-culture sourcing, including a documented history of physical altercations consistent with that political identity. This is a specific and direct characterisation, not an inferred one — notably, an earlier working assumption in this project's research had incorrectly placed Sporting Gijon's ultras scene as left-leaning based on the region's mining-industrial history, which this specific group-level finding corrected; regional working-class history does not reliably predict an individual ultras group's actual documented politics, which is exactly why this project moved toward group-specific research rather than regional inference. Ultra Boys remains Sporting Gijon's main organised ultras group.

name: Eibar
political_explanation: Eibar's Eskozia la Brava is explicitly documented as extreme-left and abertzale (Basque/Navarrese left-nationalist) in dedicated Spanish ultras-culture sourcing, placing Eibar, based in Gipuzkoa in the Basque Country, within the same well-established left-Basque-nationalist political current documented at Athletic Bilbao, Real Sociedad, Osasuna, and Alaves. As with those clubs, the explicit and specific 'abertzale' characterisation, rather than a vaguer regional-identity inference, supports the Hardcore coding. Eskozia la Brava remains Eibar's main organised ultras group. This extends the pattern of Basque-nationalist ultras politics to a smaller, less nationally prominent club, showing the political current is tied to the Basque Country region broadly rather than only its largest clubs.

name: Cadiz
political_explanation: Cadiz's Brigada Amarilla is one of Spain's oldest self-identified left-leaning ultras groups, maintaining documented informal solidarity ties with other left-wing Spanish ultras scenes, including Rayo's Bukaneros and the Basque abertzale groups, which reinforces its own political character through its choice of alliances as well as its direct self-identification. Longevity plus a consistent pattern of left-aligned inter-group solidarity supports the Hardcore coding here, rather than resting on a single source's characterisation alone. Brigada Amarilla remains Cadiz's principal organised ultras group. This entry sits alongside Bukaneros and the Basque groups as part of a recognisable, mutually-reinforcing network of self-identified left-wing Spanish ultras groups rather than an isolated case.

name: Albacete

name: Real Sociedad B
political_explanation: [Reserve team of Real Sociedad, inherits the same categorization.] Real Sociedad's Herri Norte Taldea carries a left-Basque nationalist identity tied to the broader Basque independence movement, historically even more overtly linked to Basque separatist politics than city rival Athletic Bilbao's equivalent group. This is a core, decades-old identity rather than a recent or contested characterisation — Basque left-nationalist (abertzale) ultras culture is one of the most consistently and multiply documented political currents in Spanish football, also seen at Athletic Bilbao, Osasuna, and Alaves. Herri Norte Taldea remains Real Sociedad's principal ultras group today. The Hardcore coding reflects both the consistency of the sourcing and the explicit, long-standing nature of the group's political identity, not a single incident or recent shift.

name: Real Valladolid
political_explanation: Real Valladolid's historic ultras scene split explicitly along political lines in 1996: Ultras Violetas, documented as the club's far-right ('ultraderecha') group, and Fossa Garrafoni, an explicitly far-left splinter, developed in open rivalry before both were eventually dissolved. This categorization reflects Ultras Violetas specifically, as the historically dominant and more extensively documented of the two — a case where the political division ran directly through the fanbase itself rather than existing as a single group's ideology. Given both groups are now dissolved, this describes Valladolid's ultras history rather than a currently active political identity, which is worth keeping in mind when reading this entry.

name: ADO Den Haag
political_explanation: ADO Den Haag's hardcore support (North Side) has faced repeated, well-documented controversies over racist chanting and far-right-linked hooligan incidents. This is substantially strengthened by very recent, specific evidence: a banner from ADO Den Haag's own stadium was identified among rioters at a September 2025 far-right riot in The Hague, where participants made Nazi salutes and marched alongside the Dutch intelligence service's officially classified far-right organisation, Defend Netherlands. The combination of a longer pattern of incidents plus this specific, recent, multiply-reported event supports the Hardcore coding — this is not a single ambiguous incident but a sustained pattern culminating in direct participation in an organised far-right political demonstration.

name: Ajax
political_explanation: Ahead of the November 2024 Ajax-Maccabi Tel Aviv match, F-Side stated that 'politics and football should be kept separate' while simultaneously declaring intent to physically intervene against a planned pro-Palestinian demonstration near the stadium. Opposing one side of a live political conflict is itself a political act, regardless of the group's own framing — this is a closer interpretive call than most entries in this dataset, and is flagged as such rather than presented as a settled fact. It doesn't meet the documented-violence/hate-speech bar used for Hardcore entries elsewhere, but a one-sided intervention against pro-Palestinian expression, in the context of Ajax fans' self-adopted Jewish/Israeli identity during the Gaza war, is coded as a right-leaning action.

name: FC Porto
political_explanation: A named far-right faction within Porto's Super Dragoes claque, 'Divisao Azul,' is documented by Portuguese sports journalism, distinct from the group's own public position, which does not publicly claim a political stance according to the same source. This is the same evidentiary structure as Inter's Boys San, Sporting's Grupo 1143, and Benfica's No Name Boys' section: a documented, named far-right faction operating within a broader group whose stated identity is not itself far-right. Super Dragoes remains Porto's main and currently active organised claque. Coded Faction rather than excluded, consistent with how the same kind of evidence is treated for the other three Portuguese/Italian entries in this dataset.

name: Sporting CP
political_explanation: Sporting's historic claque, Juventude Leonina (Juve Leo), once hosted 'Grupo 1143,' an explicitly far-right, neo-Nazi/skinhead FACTION led by a Hammerskins Portugal figure, documented by Portuguese press and a dedicated Wikipedia article on the group specifically; a 2005/2007 police intelligence report separately noted far-right elements generally within Juve Leo. Grupo 1143 itself was reported dormant by 2015, so this describes documented history rather than necessarily current composition. Coded Faction because the evidence is specifically about a named sub-group, not Juve Leo's stated position as a whole. Sporting's current officially registered claque, Brigada Ultras Sporting, carries no comparable documented political finding of its own.

name: SL Benfica
political_explanation: A member interview confirms swastika flags have been displayed within No Name Boys' section, hate symbolism satisfying the standard regardless of the group's formal self-identification, but this is section/faction-level evidence rather than a whole-group finding: the interviewed member explicitly denied a whole-group ideology, and a 2005/2007 Portuguese police intelligence report separately noted far-right elements generally within Benfica's claques rather than confirming a single unified position. Coded Faction for consistency with Inter's Boys San and Sporting's Grupo 1143, which rest on the same kind of evidence: a documented, named far-right presence bounded to part of a group rather than reflecting its whole, stated identity.
